title: WebSocket Frames: Opcodes, Masking & Close Codes
description: Below the handshake lies the WebSocket wire format (RFC 6455): the frame header, opcodes, mandatory client masking and why it exists, payload-length encoding, fragmentation, control frames, and close codes. The masking and length facts here are verified in Node.

questions.title: A WebSocket frame's first byte is `0x81`. What does that tell you?
questions.description: Decode the most significant byte of the frame header.

options.text: It is the **final** fragment (`FIN=1`) of a **text** message (opcode `0x1`).
It is a masked text frame.
It is the first fragment of a binary message.
It is a close control frame.

questions.explanation:
The first byte packs four fields: `FIN` (1 bit), three reserved bits `RSV1–3` (normally 0), and a 4-bit `opcode`. `0x81` is `1000 0001` in binary — `FIN=1`, `RSV=000`, `opcode=0001`. Opcode `0x1` is a text frame, and `FIN=1` means this is the last (here, the only) fragment of the message.

Masking is signalled by a *different* byte — the high bit of the **second** byte — so the first byte alone says nothing about masking. For contrast, a binary first fragment would be `0x02` (`FIN=0`, opcode `0x2`), and a close frame would be `0x88` (`FIN=1`, opcode `0x8`).

questions.title: Which opcode identifies a **binary** data frame? Give it as a decimal number.
questions.description: The opcode is the low 4 bits of the frame's first byte.
questions.explanation: The opcode registry: `0x0` continuation, `0x1` text, `0x2` binary, `0x8` close, `0x9` ping, `0xA` pong. Binary is `0x2` — decimal **2**. Binary frames carry arbitrary bytes (images, Protobuf, audio), whereas text frames (`0x1`) are constrained to valid UTF-8. The remaining opcode values are reserved for future use.

questions.title: Which statements about WebSocket frame masking are correct?

options.text: Every client→server frame must be masked using a 32-bit masking key.
Server→client frames must NOT be masked.
Unmasking is `payload[i] XOR key[i % 4]`, applied byte by byte.
Masking encrypts the payload, so `wss://` is unnecessary.

questions.title: What problem does mandatory client→server masking actually solve?
questions.description: It isn't secrecy — the key is sent in the clear. So why require it?

options.text: It stops malicious page scripts from crafting byte sequences that poison non-compliant intermediary proxies and caches.
It encrypts the payload end-to-end.
It compresses the payload to save bandwidth.
It detects transmission bit-errors like a checksum.

questions.explanation:
Masking is a **security mitigation for intermediaries**, not for the endpoints. The threat: a script in a browser could deliberately send bytes that a buggy or transparent proxy might misread as a *second*, attacker-chosen HTTP request — enabling cache poisoning or request smuggling. Masking every frame with an unpredictable, per-frame random key makes the on-the-wire bytes impossible for the script to control, defeating that attack.

It is explicitly *not* about the other three: no confidentiality (XOR with a clear-text key), no compression (that's the optional `permessage-deflate` extension), and no error detection (TCP already guarantees integrity).

questions.title: A frame's 7-bit payload-length field holds the value `126`. What does that mean?
questions.description: Recall how WebSocket encodes payloads of differing sizes.

options.text: It's an escape code: the actual length is read from the next 2 bytes as a 16-bit unsigned integer.
The payload is exactly 126 bytes long.
The payload is 126 kibibytes long.
126 is the length of the masking key.

questions.explanation:
The base length field is 7 bits, so it natively expresses 0–125. Two values are escapes: `126` means "read the next **2** bytes as a 16-bit length" (up to 65 535), and `127` means "read the next **8** bytes as a 64-bit length." So `126` is never a literal size — it signals that the extended 16-bit field follows.

```text
len 0–125  → that value IS the length
len 126    → next 2 bytes  = length (16-bit)
len 127    → next 8 bytes  = length (64-bit)
```

This variable-length scheme keeps small frames tiny (a single length byte) while still allowing very large payloads.

questions.title: Name the control frame (opcode `0x8`) that initiates the WebSocket closing handshake.
questions.description: One or two words. The peer echoes it before the TCP connection ends.
questions.explanation: The **Close** frame (opcode `0x8`) starts the closing handshake. It may carry a 2-byte status code followed by an optional UTF-8 reason. The peer replies with its own Close frame (echoing or choosing a code), and only then is the TCP connection torn down. That two-way Close exchange is what makes a shutdown *clean*; if the socket simply drops without it, the API reports an *abnormal* closure instead.

questions.title: Which rules apply to WebSocket control frames (Close, Ping, Pong)?
questions.description: Select every true statement about control frames.

options.text: Their payload must be 125 bytes or fewer.
They must not be fragmented — `FIN` must be 1.
They may be injected between the fragments of a larger data message.
Like data frames, a control frame may be split across multiple continuation frames.

questions.explanation: Control frames are identified by opcodes `0x8` (close), `0x9` (ping), and `0xA` (pong). The spec constrains them tightly so a peer can always process them promptly: payload ≤ **125 bytes** (which is also why they always fit in the 7-bit length with no escape), and they are **never fragmented** (`FIN=1` always). Importantly, a control frame *may* appear in the middle of a fragmented data message — so a Ping can be answered even while a large file is still mid-transfer. The false option inverts the no-fragmentation rule.

questions.title: What WebSocket close status code signals a **normal** closure?
questions.description: The code a peer sends in a Close frame when everything finished cleanly.
questions.explanation:
**1000** = Normal Closure: the connection fulfilled its purpose. Other common codes: `1001` going away (server shutting down or page navigating away), `1002` protocol error, `1003` unsupported data, `1009` message too big, `1011` server-side internal error. Application-defined codes live in the `4000–4999` range.

WebSocket close codes start at 1000 (not in HTTP's 1xx/2xx ranges) precisely because they describe a *separate* protocol concern from the HTTP handshake that bootstrapped the connection.

questions.title: Your client logs WebSocket close code `1006`, but the server insists it never sent it. Why?
questions.description: 1006 means "Abnormal Closure." So who sets it?

options.text: `1006` is reserved for local use — it means the connection dropped with no Close frame at all, so the endpoint's API sets it and it never travels on the wire.
`1006` is browser-only; Node servers send `1000` in its place.
`1006` actually means normal closure and is just an alias for `1000`.
`1006` is only emitted over `ws://`, never over `wss://`.

questions.explanation:
A few close codes are **reserved** and must never appear inside an actual Close frame: `1005` (no status code was present), `1006` (abnormal closure — the TCP connection dropped without a Close handshake), and `1015` (TLS failure). The local API surfaces them to *describe what it observed*, not because a peer transmitted them.

So `1006` is the very symptom of *not* receiving a Close frame: a network blip, a crashed peer, or a proxy/load-balancer idle-timeout. A flood of `1006`s points at infrastructure rather than application logic — the fix is Ping/Pong health-checks plus a reconnection strategy.

questions.title: A message is sent as several fragments. What opcode do the 2nd and later frames carry?
questions.description: Think about how a receiver knows where a fragmented message continues and ends.

options.text: `0x0` (continuation): only the first fragment carries the `text`/`binary` opcode; the rest are continuation frames, and the last sets `FIN=1`.
Each fragment repeats the original `text` or `binary` opcode.
`0x8` (close) is sent between each fragment.
Fragmentation isn't allowed — every message must be a single frame.

questions.explanation:
Fragmentation lets a sender start transmitting before it knows the total size. The pattern is:

```text
frame 1: FIN=0, opcode=0x1 (text)         ← first fragment names the type
frame 2: FIN=0, opcode=0x0 (continuation)
frame 3: FIN=1, opcode=0x0 (continuation) ← FIN=1 ends the message
```

The receiver reassembles the fragments in order into one logical message. Control frames (ping/pong/close) may be interleaved between data fragments, but the data fragments themselves arrive strictly in order on the single TCP connection.

questions.title: Must a `text` frame's payload be valid UTF-8, and what happens if it isn't?
questions.description: Consider the difference between text (`0x1`) and binary (`0x2`) frames.

options.text: Yes — text frames must be valid UTF-8; a receiver that detects invalid UTF-8 must fail the connection with close code `1007`.
No — text frames may carry any bytes; the application decides the encoding.
Invalid bytes are silently replaced with the U+FFFD replacement character.
Text frames are restricted to 7-bit ASCII only.

questions.explanation:
Opcode `0x1` is defined as **UTF-8 text**. Receiving invalid UTF-8 in a text frame is a protocol violation, and the endpoint must fail the connection with close code `1007` ("invalid frame payload data"). To send arbitrary bytes instead, use a **binary** frame (`0x2`), which carries any octets with no encoding constraint.

This is exactly why libraries hand you a `string` for text frames but a `Buffer`/`ArrayBuffer` for binary ones — the frame's opcode, not a guess about the contents, decides the type.
